title: Overview — Why dbt Wizard Is Different
tagline: A dbt agent that queries a live DAG graph, not files
summary: dbt Wizard is an AI agent for dbt projects, built by Fivetran and operating on dbt Labs' dbt. Instead of reading source files and grepping text, it answers questions against a live, queryable index of the project: models, columns, tests, and edges held in a local DuckDB index, joined to the warehouse as one unified graph. Every search, impact, and lineage call returns DAG context inline — parents, children, grain, PK markers, materialization, and last-run test state — because the index already reflects the actual last-run state. Interaction happens through structured MCP tool calls (mcp__dbt_index__*), not shell strings or raw file access.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent treats a dbt project as a directory of text: it opens .sql and .yml files, greps for a column name, and infers relationships from {{ ref() }} strings it happens to read. It has no notion of grain, PK, materialization, or whether a test currently passes — and a textual match for brand_name tells you nothing about blast radius. dbt Wizard instead traverses a precomputed graph where edges are real lineage, results carry severity and test state, and the warehouse is one query away. The difference is structured graph intelligence versus pattern-matching over file contents.

capabilities.title: Local DuckDB project index
capabilities.detail: Models, columns, tests, and edges are indexed in a local DuckDB store that reflects the actual last-run state: pass/fail tests, run results, timings, grain, PK markers, schemas, and materializations are known out of the box, with no file parsing at query time.

capabilities.title: Graph context on every result
capabilities.detail: search, lineage, describe, and impact don't return bare matches. Each result is decorated with its DAG neighborhood — immediate parents and children, grain, PK status, materialization, and current test state — so one call orients you instead of triggering ten follow-up file reads.

capabilities.title: Structured tool calls, not shell or grep
capabilities.detail: All interaction is through typed mcp__dbt_index__* tool calls with named arguments and structured returns. There is no grep, no cat, no fuzzy file globbing — search can run BM25 over metadata or regex over raw/compiled SQL, but always with DAG context attached.

capabilities.title: Warehouse as part of the same graph
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse runs read-only SQL directly against Snowflake/BigQuery for live previews, and dbt_show resolves full Jinja against the warehouse — so the agent reasons over both the modeled graph and live data without a dbt run.

capabilities.title: Fast project orientation
capabilities.detail: An opening 'get me oriented' prompt resolves to a handful of describe/lineage/search calls that surface layers (bronze/silver/gold), grains, PKs, materializations, and failing tests in seconds — work that would otherwise mean reading dozens of files.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__describe
tools.purpose: Full node introspection straight from the index: columns, compiled SQL, test results, lineage, and catalog types — including grain, PK markers, schema, and materialization.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model/source name to introspect, e.g. fct_orders or stg_products.

params.name: include
params.desc: Optional facets to expand: columns, compiled_sql, tests, lineage, catalog.

tools.returns: A structured node record: grain, PK column(s), materialization, schema, column list with types, last-run test pass/fail, and immediate upstream/downstream edges.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__search
tools.purpose: BM25 metadata search OR regex against raw/compiled SQL, with DAG context attached to every hit — used to locate models/columns by meaning or by literal SQL pattern.

params.name: query
params.desc: Search terms (BM25) or a regex pattern when mode=regex.

params.name: scope
params.desc: Optional subgraph/layer filter, e.g. silver or a tag/path selector.

tools.returns: Ranked matches, each with the node, the matched column/SQL span, and DAG context: parents, children, grain, materialization, and test status.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__lineage
tools.purpose: Upstream/downstream traversal with hop-depth control and edge-type filtering, scoped to a subgraph — to map how a model or column connects through the DAG.

params.name: node
params.desc: Anchor node for the traversal, e.g. dim_customers.

params.name: max_depth
params.desc: Hop limit; controls how far the traversal walks.

params.name: edge_types
params.desc: Filter to edge kinds, e.g. ref, source, column-level.

params.name: column
params.desc: Optional column to scope column-level lineage, e.g. brand_name.

tools.returns: An ordered list of nodes/edges per hop with grain and materialization on each node — a scoped subgraph rather than a flat file list.

examplePrompts.prompt: Get me oriented in this project — what are the layers, the core fact tables, their grain, and is anything failing tests right now?
examplePrompts.intent: Opening orientation; pull structure, grain, and test-state from the index instead of reading files.

examplePrompts.prompt: Describe fct_orders — I want its grain, primary key, materialization, columns, and current test status.
Where is brand_name actually produced and which downstream models consume it?
examplePrompts.intent: Column-level lineage rather than a text search for the string.

examplePrompts.prompt: Find every model in the silver layer that does a window function over order_id.
examplePrompts.intent: Regex SQL search scoped to a layer, with DAG context.

examplePrompts.prompt: Show me the full upstream chain for dim_customers, max 3 hops, ref edges only.
Which gold models depend on stg_products, and what's the grain of each?
examplePrompts.intent: Downstream traversal with grain decoration per node.

interaction.scenario: An SE opens an unfamiliar customer dbt project mid-demo and asks dbt Wizard for orientation. The goal is to show the wizard answering from the live index — grain, PKs, materializations, test state — rather than reading files.
interaction.userPrompt: Get me oriented in this project. What layers exist, what are the core fact tables and their grain, and is anything failing tests right now?

events.body: Get me oriented in this project. What layers exist, what are the core fact tables and their grain, and is anything failing tests right now?

I won't read files for this. I'll query the local DuckDB index, which already knows layers, grain, PKs, materializations, and last-run test state. Starting with a metadata search to map the layers and locate fact tables, then introspecting the core facts, then checking the failing-test surface.

events.code: {"hits":[{"node":"fct_orders","layer":"gold","materialization":"incremental","grain":"one row per order_id","pk":"order_id","children":["rpt_revenue_daily"],"tests":{"pass":7,"fail":1}},{"node":"fct_order_items","layer":"gold","materialization":"incremental","grain":"one row per order_id+product_id","pk":"order_item_id","tests":{"pass":5,"fail":0}},{"node":"int_orders_enriched","layer":"silver","materialization":"view","grain":"one row per order_id","tests":{"pass":4,"fail":0}}],"layers_seen":["bronze","silver","gold"]}
events.body: Three layers detected (bronze/silver/gold). Two gold facts surfaced with grain, PK, and test counts inline. fct_orders shows 1 failing test.
Every hit already carries grain, PK, materialization, and test status — no follow-up file reads needed. fct_orders has a failing test; I'll introspect it fully to name the failure and confirm grain.
Failing test: not_null_order_total, 214 failing rows. Parents: int_orders_enriched, dim_customers. Child: rpt_revenue_daily.
The null order_total is in a gold fact, so I want to know what consumes it before flagging severity. One scoped downstream traversal.
order_total feeds rpt_revenue_daily (daily revenue) and on to a Looker revenue dashboard exposure.

Orientation, pulled live from the project index (no files read):

Layers: bronze, silver, gold.

Core gold facts:
- fct_orders — incremental, schema analytics_gold, grain one row per order_id (PK order_id). Parents: int_orders_enriched, dim_customers.
- fct_order_items — incremental, grain one row per order_id + product_id (PK order_item_id), all tests passing.

Silver staging of note: int_orders_enriched — view, grain one row per order_id, tests passing.

Failing test right now: fct_orders.not_null_order_total — 214 null order_total rows. That column flows downstream into rpt_revenue_daily (daily revenue) and into the looker_revenue_dashboard exposure, so the nulls are reaching a customer-facing revenue report. That's the first thing I'd chase. Want me to run an impact report on order_total, or preview the offending rows against the warehouse?

seNotes: Lead with the contrast: a general coding agent greps file text for brand_name and infers ref() relationships from strings it happens to open. dbt Wizard answers from a precomputed graph, so one orientation prompt returns grain, PK, materialization, and live test state instead of triggering a dozen file reads.

Make the 'no file reads' point explicit on stage. The index is a local DuckDB store of models/columns/tests/edges reflecting last-run state; that's why grain and pass/fail tests are known instantly without parsing .sql or .yml.

Show that results carry graph context inline. After the search hit, do NOT open a file — point out parents, children, grain, and test counts are already in the result payload. That's the whole thesis in one frame.

Emphasize structured tool calls over shell: all calls are typed mcp__dbt_index__* with named args. search can do BM25 metadata OR regex over compiled SQL, but it's never bare grep — DAG context is always attached.

Tee up the warehouse-as-one-graph follow-up (warehouse read-only preview or dbt_show with full Jinja) so the audience sees the modeled graph and live data are reachable in the same session without a full dbt run.
